revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''諸蕃'''（しょばん）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]においていわゆる「[[三韓]]」と呼ばれた[[朝鮮半島]]の国々及びそこに出自...'
revision.text:
'''諸蕃'''（しょばん）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]においていわゆる「[[三韓]]」と呼ばれた[[朝鮮半島]]の国々及びそこに出自を持つ[[渡来人]]系[[氏族]]を指す言葉。[[和訓]]は「となりのくに」。
古代[[中国]]において周辺諸国を「化外」という意味で「'''蕃'''」と呼称したものが、日本でもこれを模倣して用いたと考えられている。
『[[日本書紀]]』[[清寧天皇]]3年11月条（[[482年]]）以下複数の記事が見られ、[[律令制]]でも「[[玄蕃寮]]」が設置されるなど、「諸蕃」・「蕃」・「蕃国」の表記がしばしば用いられた。ただし、大国である中国（[[唐]]）に対しては同じ和訓を持つ「隣国」という表記が公式には用いられた。また、[[7世紀]]後半に[[新羅]]が朝鮮半島を統一すると、対象国が事実上同国のみとなってしまったために新羅を指して蕃国と呼ぶ例も現れる。渡来人としての「諸蕃」の使用の始期については不明な点も多いが、『日本書紀』の注釈書である「弘仁私記」には、当時『諸蕃雑姓記』という書物があったことが記されている。確実に判明するものでは、[[弘仁]]6年（[[815年]]）に成立した『新撰姓氏録』であり、「皇別」・「神別」と並んで「諸蕃」（「蕃別」ではない）という出自呼称が用いられ、「大漢（中国）三韓の族」を意味するものと定義されている。
== 参考文献 == *佐伯有清「諸蕃」（『日本史大事典 3』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1）
{{DEFAULTSORT:しよはん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

'''諸蕃'''（しょばん）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]においていわゆる「[[三韓]]」と呼ばれた[[朝鮮半島]]の国々及びそこに出自を持つ[[渡来人]]系[[氏族]]を指す言葉。広義では[[中国大陸]]出身者を含む場合もある。[[和訓]]は「となりのくに」。
古代[[中国]]において周辺諸国を「化外」という意味で「'''蕃'''」と呼称したものが、日本でもこれを模倣して用いたと考えられている。
『[[日本書紀]]』[[清寧天皇]]3年11月条（[[482年]]）以下複数の記事が見られ、[[律令制]]でも「[[玄蕃寮]]」が設置されるなど、「諸蕃」・「蕃」・「蕃国」の表記がしばしば用いられた。ただし、大国である中国（[[唐]]）に対しては同じ和訓を持つ「隣国」という表記が公式には用いられた。また、[[7世紀]]後半に[[新羅]]が朝鮮半島を統一すると、対象国が事実上同国のみとなってしまったために新羅を指して蕃国と呼ぶ例も現れる。渡来人としての「諸蕃」の使用の始期については不明な点も多いが、『日本書紀』の注釈書である「弘仁私記」には、当時『諸蕃雑姓記』という書物があったことが記されている。確実に判明するものでは、[[弘仁]]6年（[[815年]]）に成立した『新撰姓氏録』であり、「皇別」・「神別」と並んで「諸蕃」（「蕃別」ではない）という出自呼称が用いられ、「大漢（中国）三韓の族」を意味するものと定義されている。
== 参考文献 == *佐伯有清「諸蕃」（『日本史大事典 3』（平凡社、1993年） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1）
{{DEFAULTSORT:しよはん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

'''諸蕃'''（しょばん）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]においていわゆる「[[三韓]]」と呼ばれた[[朝鮮半島]]の国々及びそこに出自を持つ[[渡来人]]系[[氏族]]を指す言葉。広義では[[中国大陸]]出身者を含む場合もある。[[和訓]]は「となりのくに」。
古代[[中国]]において周辺諸国を「化外」という意味で「'''蕃'''」と呼称したものが、日本でもこれを模倣して用いたと考えられている。
『[[日本書紀]]』[[清寧天皇]]3年11月条（[[482年]]）以下複数の記事が見られ、[[律令制]]でも「[[玄蕃寮]]」が設置されるなど、「諸蕃」・「蕃」・「蕃国」の表記がしばしば用いられた。ただし、大国である中国（[[唐]]）に対しては同じ和訓を持つ「隣国」という表記が公式には用いられた。また、[[7世紀]]後半に[[新羅]]が朝鮮半島を統一すると、対象国が事実上同国のみとなってしまったために新羅を指して蕃国と呼ぶ例も現れる。渡来人としての「諸蕃」の使用の始期については不明な点も多いが、『日本書紀』の注釈書である「弘仁私記」には、当時『諸蕃雑姓記』という書物があったことが記されている。確実に判明するものでは、[[弘仁]]6年（[[815年]]）に成立した『[[新撰姓氏録]]』であり、「皇別」・「神別」と並んで「諸蕃」（「蕃別」ではない）という出自呼称が用いられ、「大漢（中国）三韓の族」を意味するものと定義されている。
== 参考文献 == * 佐伯有清「諸蕃」（『日本史大事典 3』（[[平凡社]]、[[1993年]]） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1）
{{DEFAULTSORT:しよはん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

'''諸蕃'''（しょばん）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]においていわゆる「[[三韓]]」と呼ばれた[[朝鮮半島]]の国々及びそこに出自を持つ[[渡来人]]系[[氏族]]を指す言葉。広義では[[中国大陸]]出身者を含む場合もある。[[和訓]]は「となりのくに」。「もろもろのえびす」。
古代[[中国]]において周辺諸国を「化外」という意味で「'''蕃'''」と呼称したものが、日本でもこれを模倣して用いたと考えられている。
『[[日本書紀]]』[[清寧天皇]]3年11月条（[[482年]]）以下複数の記事が見られ、[[律令制]]でも「[[玄蕃寮]]」が設置されるなど、「諸蕃」・「蕃」・「蕃国」の表記がしばしば用いられた。ただし、大国である中国（[[唐]]）に対しては同じ和訓を持つ「隣国」という表記が公式には用いられた。また、[[7世紀]]後半に[[新羅]]が朝鮮半島を統一すると、対象国が事実上同国のみとなってしまったために新羅を指して蕃国と呼ぶ例も現れる。渡来人としての「諸蕃」の使用の始期については不明な点も多いが、『日本書紀』の注釈書である「弘仁私記」には、当時『諸蕃雑姓記』という書物があったことが記されている。確実に判明するものでは、[[弘仁]]6年（[[815年]]）に成立した『[[新撰姓氏録]]』であり、「皇別」・「神別」と並んで「諸蕃」（「蕃別」ではない）という出自呼称が用いられ、「大漢（中国）三韓の族」を意味するものと定義されている。

{{DEFAULTSORT:しよはん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]] [[Category:渡来人|*しよはん]]

'''諸蕃'''（しょばん）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における用語で、「[[三韓]]」と呼ばれた[[朝鮮半島]]の国々及び高句麗(こうくり)、百済(くだら)、新羅(しらぎ)など朝鮮諸国に対する呼び名。[[和訓]]は「となりのくに」。「もろもろのえびす」。また、[[中国大陸]]および[[三韓]]に出自を持つ[[渡来人]]系[[氏族]]を指す言葉としても用いられた。[[秦氏]]、[[東漢氏]]、[[百済王氏]]などが知られる。
古代[[中国]]において周辺諸国を「化外」という意味で「'''蕃'''」と呼称したものが、日本でもこれを模倣して用いたと考えられている。
『[[日本書紀]]』[[清寧天皇]]3年11月条（[[482年]]）以下複数の記事が見られ、[[律令制]]でも「[[玄蕃寮]]」が設置されるなど、「諸蕃」・「蕃」・「蕃国」の表記がしばしば用いられた。ただし、大国である中国（[[唐]]）に対しては同じ和訓を持つ「隣国」という表記が公式には用いられた。また、[[7世紀]]後半に[[新羅]]が朝鮮半島を統一すると、対象国が事実上同国のみとなってしまったために新羅を指して蕃国と呼ぶ例も現れる。渡来人としての「諸蕃」の使用の始期については不明な点も多いが、『日本書紀』の注釈書である「弘仁私記」には、当時『諸蕃雑姓記』という書物があったことが記されている。確実に判明するものでは、[[弘仁]]6年（[[815年]]）に成立した『[[新撰姓氏録]]』であり、「[[皇別]]」・「[[神別]]」と並んで「諸蕃」（「蕃別」ではない）という出自呼称が用いられ、「大漢（中国）三韓の族」を意味するものと定義されている。[[明治時代]]に成立した[[華族]]の宗族制度では、諸蕃の家系の家は「外別」として扱われている。
== 参考文献 == * [[佐伯有清]]「諸蕃」（『日本史大事典 3』（[[平凡社]]、[[1993年]]） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1） * 佐伯有清「諸蕃」『[[日本大百科全書]]』（[[小学館]]<ref>[[コトバンク]][https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%B8%E8%95%83-535074]</ref>）
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[新撰姓氏録]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:しよはん}} [[Category:日本の律令制]] [[Category:渡来人|*しよはん]]

'''諸蕃'''（しょばん）とは、[[古代]][[日本]]における用語で、「[[三韓]]」と呼ばれた[[朝鮮半島]]の国々及び高句麗(こうくり)、百済(くだら)、新羅(しらぎ)など朝鮮諸国に対する呼び名。[[和訓]]は「となりのくに」。「もろもろのえびす」。また、[[中国大陸]]および[[三韓]]に出自を持つ[[渡来人]]系[[氏族]]を指す言葉としても用いられた。[[秦氏]]、[[東漢氏]]、[[百済王氏]]などが知られる。
古代[[中国]]において周辺諸国を「化外」という意味で「'''蕃'''」と呼称したものが、日本でもこれに倣って用いたと考えられている。
『[[日本書紀]]』[[清寧天皇]]3年11月条（[[482年]]）以下複数の記事が見られ、[[律令制]]でも「[[玄蕃寮]]」が設置されるなど、「諸蕃」・「蕃」・「蕃国」の表記がしばしば用いられた。ただし、大国である中国（[[唐]]）に対しては同じ和訓を持つ「隣国」という表記が公式には用いられた。また、[[7世紀]]後半に[[新羅]]が朝鮮半島を統一すると、対象国が事実上同国のみとなってしまったために新羅を指して蕃国と呼ぶ例も現れる。渡来人としての「諸蕃」の使用の始期については不明な点も多いが、『日本書紀』の注釈書である「弘仁私記」には、当時『諸蕃雑姓記』という書物があったことが記されている。確実に判明するものでは、[[弘仁]]6年（[[815年]]）に成立した『[[新撰姓氏録]]』であり、「[[皇別]]」・「[[神別]]」と並んで「諸蕃」（「蕃別」ではない）という出自呼称が用いられ、「大漢（中国）三韓の族」を意味するものと定義されている。[[明治時代]]に成立した[[華族]]の宗族制度では、諸蕃の家系の家は「外別」として扱われている。
== 参考文献 == * [[佐伯有清]]「諸蕃」（『日本史大事典 3』（[[平凡社]]、[[1993年]]） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1） * 佐伯有清「諸蕃」『[[日本大百科全書]]』（[[小学館]]<ref>[[コトバンク]][https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%B8%E8%95%83-535074]</ref>）
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[新撰姓氏録]]

『[[日本書紀]]』[[清寧天皇]]3年11月条（[[482年]]）以下複数の記事が見られ、[[律令制]]でも「[[玄蕃寮]]」が設置されるなど、「諸蕃」・「蕃」・「蕃国」の表記がしばしば用いられた。ただし、大国である中国（[[唐]]）に対しては同じ和訓を持つ「隣国」という表記が公式には用いられた。また、[[7世紀]]後半に[[新羅]]が朝鮮半島を統一すると、対象国が事実上同国のみとなってしまったために新羅を指して蕃国と呼ぶ例も現れる。渡来人としての「諸蕃」の使用の始期については不明な点も多いが、『日本書紀』の注釈書である「弘仁私記」には、当時『諸蕃雑姓記』という書物があったことが記されている。確実に判明するものでは、[[弘仁]]6年（[[815年]]）に成立した『[[新撰姓氏録]]』であり、「[[皇別]]」・「[[神別]]」と並んで「諸蕃」（「蕃別」ではない）という出自呼称が用いられ、「大漢（中国）三韓の族」を意味するものと定義されている。[[明治時代]]に成立した[[華族]]の[[宗族制 (華族)|宗族制度]]では、諸蕃の家系の家は「外別」として扱われている。
== 参考文献 == * [[佐伯有清]]「諸蕃」（『日本史大事典 3』（[[平凡社]]、[[1993年]]） ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1） * 佐伯有清「諸蕃」『[[日本大百科全書]]』（[[小学館]]<ref>[[コトバンク]][https://kotobank.jp/word/%E8%AB%B8%E8%95%83-535074]</ref>）
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[新撰姓氏録]] * [[騎馬民族]]
* [[諸蕃志]] - 中国の地理書。

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Shoban''' (諸蕃) is a term used in [[ancient]] [[Japan]] to refer to the countries of the [[Korean Peninsula]] known as the [[Three Han]]—including [[Goguryeo]] (高句麗, Kōkuri), [[Baekje]] (百済, Kudara), and [[Silla]] (新羅, Shiragi)—as well as China. The [[Wakun]] (Japanese reading) for this term is "tonari no kuni" ("neighboring countries") or "moromoro no ebisu" ("various barbarians"). The term was also used to refer to [[immigrant clans]] of Tora..."

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh_min_nan:Chu-hoan]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11632144]] Wikidata name: Shoban [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shoban''' (諸蕃) is a term used in [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]] to refer to the countries of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] known as the [[:en:Three Han|Three Han]]—including [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] (高句麗, Kōkuri), [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (百済, Kudara), and [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (新羅, Shiragi)—as well as China. The [[Wakun]] (Japanese reading) for this term is "tonari no kuni" ("neighboring countries") or "moromoro no ebisu" ("various barbarians"). The term was also used to refer to [[immigrant clans]] of [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]] origin from the Three Han, such as the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] and the [[:en:Kudara no Konikishi clan|Baekje no Ōkimi clan]].
Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]],'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Emperor Seinei]]'s reign ([[:en:482|482]]). During the [[Ritsuryō system]], administrative offices like the ''{{ill|Genbanryō|ja|玄蕃寮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbanryō|qq=|1=Genbanryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572314}}'' were established, and terms such as "Shoban," "Ban," and "Bankoku" were frequently used. After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the latter half of the [[:en:7th century|7th century]], "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). The earliest confirmed instance is in the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]],'' compiled in [[:en:815|815]] during the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], where "Shoban" (not "Banbetsu") was used as a designation alongside ''{{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|1=Shinbetsu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}}.'' It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|qq=|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Arikiyo Saeki]] "Shoban" (in ''{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], [[:en:1993|1993]]) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * {{Kotobank}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] * [[:en:Eurasian nomads|Mounted archery people]]

After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the latter half of the [[:en:7th century|7th century]], "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|qq=|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh-min-nan:Chu-hoan]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11632144]] Wikidata name: Shoban [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shoban''' (諸蕃) is a term used in [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]] to refer to the countries of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] known as the [[:en:Three Han|Three Han]]—including [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] (高句麗, Kōkuri), [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (百済, Kudara), and [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (新羅, Shiragi)—as well as China. The [[Wakun]] (Japanese reading) for this term is "tonari no kuni" ("neighboring countries") or "moromoro no ebisu" ("various barbarians"). The term was also used to refer to [[immigrant clans]] of [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]] origin from the Three Han, such as the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] and the [[:en:Kudara no Konikishi clan|Baekje no Ōkimi clan]].
Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]],'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Emperor Seinei]]'s reign ([[:en:482|482]]). During the [[Ritsuryō system]], administrative offices like the ''{{ill|Genbanryō|ja|玄蕃寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbanryō|1=Genbanryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572314}}'' were established, and terms such as "Shoban," "Ban," and "Bankoku" were frequently used. After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the latter half of the [[:en:7th century|7th century]], "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). The earliest confirmed instance is in the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]],'' compiled in [[:en:815|815]] during the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], where "Shoban" (not "Banbetsu") was used as a designation alongside ''{{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|1=Shinbetsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}}.'' It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh-min-nan:Chu-hoan]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11632144]] Wikidata name: Shoban [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shoban''' (諸蕃) is a term used in [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]] to refer to the countries of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] known as the [[:en:Three Han|Three Han]]—including [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] (高句麗, Kōkuri), [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (百済, Kudara), and [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (新羅, Shiragi)—as well as China. The [[Wakun]] (Japanese reading) for this term is "tonari no kuni" ("neighboring countries") or "moromoro no ebisu" ("various barbarians"). The term was also used to refer to [[immigrant clans]] of [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]] origin from the Three Han, such as the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] and the [[:en:Kudara no Konikishi clan|Baekje no Ōkimi clan]].
Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]],'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Emperor Seinei]]'s reign ([[:en:482|482]]). During the [[Ritsuryō system]], administrative offices like the ''{{ill|Genbanryō|ja|玄蕃寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbanryō|1=Genbanryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572314}}'' were established, and terms such as "Shoban," "Ban," and "Bankoku" were frequently used. After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the latter half of the [[:en:7th century|7th century]], "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). The earliest confirmed instance is in the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]],'' compiled in [[:en:815|815]] during the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], where "Shoban" (not "Banbetsu") was used as a designation alongside ''{{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|1=Shinbetsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}}.'' It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh-min-nan:Chu-hoan]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11632144]] Wikidata name: Shoban [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shoban''' (諸蕃) is a term used in [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]] to refer to the countries of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] known as the [[:en:Three Han|Three Han]]—including [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] (高句麗, Kōkuri), [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (百済, Kudara), and [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (新羅, Shiragi)—as well as China. The [[Wakun]] (Japanese reading) for this term is "tonari no kuni" ("neighboring countries") or "moromoro no ebisu" ("various barbarians"). The term was also used to refer to [[immigrant clans]] of [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]] origin from the Three Han, such as the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] and the [[:en:Kudara no Konikishi clan|Baekje no Ōkimi clan]].
Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]],'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Emperor Seinei]]'s reign ([[:en:482|482]]). During the [[Ritsuryō system]], administrative offices like the ''{{ill|Genbanryō|ja|玄蕃寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbanryō|1=Genbanryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572314}}'' were established, and terms such as "Shoban," "Ban," and "Bankoku" were frequently used. After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the latter half of the [[:en:7th century|7th century]], "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). The earliest confirmed instance is in the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]],'' compiled in [[:en:815|815]] during the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], where "Shoban" (not "Banbetsu") was used as a designation alongside ''{{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|1=Shinbetsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}}.'' It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").
== References == {{reflist}}
== Sources == * [[Arikiyo Saeki]] "Shoban" (in ''{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], [[:en:1993|1993]]) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * {{Kotobank}}
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]] * [[:en:Eurasian nomads|Mounted archery people]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|諸蕃|version=94670561|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories|
[[Category:Completion tier 6]]
}}

It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh_min_nan:Chu-hoan]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh_min_nan:Chu-hoan]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh-min-nan:Chu-hoan]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11632144]] Wikidata name: Shoban [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Shoban''' (諸蕃) is a term used in [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient history]] [[:en:Japan|Japan]] to refer to the countries of the [[:en:Korea|Korea]] known as the [[:en:Three Han|Three Han]]—including [[:en:Goguryeo|Goguryeo]] (高句麗, Kōkuri), [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (百済, Kudara), and [[:en:Silla|Silla]] (新羅, Shiragi)—as well as China. The [[Wakun]] (Japanese reading) for this term is "tonari no kuni" ("neighboring countries") or "moromoro no ebisu" ("various barbarians"). The term was also used to refer to [[immigrant clans]] of [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]] origin from the Three Han, such as the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] and the [[:en:Kudara no Konikishi clan|Baekje no Ōkimi clan]].
Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]],'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Emperor Seinei]]'s reign ([[:en:482|482]]). During the [[Ritsuryō system]], administrative offices like the ''{{ill|Genbanryō|ja|玄蕃寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbanryō|1=Genbanryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572314}}'' were established, and terms such as "Shoban," "Ban," and "Bankoku" were frequently used. After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the latter half of the [[:en:7th century|7th century]], "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). The earliest confirmed instance is in the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]],'' compiled in [[:en:815|815]] during the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], where "Shoban" (not "Banbetsu") was used as a designation alongside ''{{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|1=Shinbetsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}}.'' It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]],'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Emperor Seinei]]'s reign ([[:en:482|482]]). During the [[Ritsuryō system]], administrative offices like the ''{{ill|Genbanryō|ja|玄蕃寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbanryō|1=Genbanryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572314}}'' were established, and terms such as "Shoban," "Ban," and "Bankoku" were frequently used. After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by [[:en:Silla|Silla]] in the latter half of the [[:en:7th century|7th century]], "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). The earliest confirmed instance is in the ''[[:en:Shinsen Shōjiroku|Shinsen Shōjiroku]],'' compiled in [[:en:815|815]] during the [[:en:Kōnin (era)|Kōnin era]], where "Shoban" (not "Banbetsu") was used as a designation alongside ''{{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|1=Shinbetsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}}.'' It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

== Sources == * [[Arikiyo Saeki]] "Shoban" (in ''{{ill|Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja|日本史大事典 3|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nihonshi Daijiten 3|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'' ([[:en:Heibonsha|Heibonsha]], [[:en:1993|1993]]) ISBN 978-4-582-13103-1) * {{Kotobank}}

The [[Wakun]] (Japanese reading) for this term is "tonari no kuni" ("neighboring countries") or "moromoro no ebisu" ("various barbarians"). The term was also used to refer to [[immigrant clans]] of [[:en:Toraijin|Toraijin]] origin from the Three Han, such as the [[:en:Hata clan|Hata clan]] and the [[:en:Kudara no Konikishi clan|Baekje no Ōkimi clan]].
Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''[[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]],'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of [[:en:Emperor Seinei|Emperor Seinei]]'s reign ([[:en:482|482]]). It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}},'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Seinei|en|Emperor Seinei|WD=Q357273|lt=Emperor Seinei}}'s reign ({{ill|482|en|482|WD=Q32534|lt=482}}). During the [[Ritsuryō system]], administrative offices like the ''{{ill|Genbanryō|ja|玄蕃寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbanryō|1=Genbanryō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572314}}'' were established, and terms such as "Shoban," "Ban," and "Bankoku" were frequently used. After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla}} in the latter half of the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century}}, "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). The earliest confirmed instance is in the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku}},'' compiled in {{ill|815|en|815|WD=Q29917|lt=815}} during the {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin era}}, where "Shoban" (not "Banbetsu") was used as a designation alongside ''{{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|1=Shinbetsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}}.'' It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}}, under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Clan system (Kazoku)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh-min-nan:Chu-hoan]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh-min-nan:Chu-hoan]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:諸蕃]][[ko:제번]][[zh-min-nan:Chu-hoan]]
'''Shoban''' (諸蕃) is a term used in {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=Ancient history|1=Ancient history}} {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} to refer to the countries of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|WD=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}} known as the [[:en:Three Han|Three Han]]—including {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|WD=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} (高句麗, Kōkuri), [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]] (百済, Kudara), and {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} (新羅, Shiragi)—as well as China. The [[Wakun]] (Japanese reading) for this term is "tonari no kuni" ("neighboring countries") or "moromoro no ebisu" ("various barbarians"). The term was also used to refer to [[immigrant clans]] of {{ill|Toraijin|en|Toraijin|WD=Q11561926|lt=Toraijin|1=Toraijin}} origin from the Three Han, such as the {{ill|Hata clan|en|Hata clan|WD=Q987290|lt=Hata clan|1=Hata clan}} and the {{ill|Kudara no Konikishi clan|en|Kudara no Konikishi clan|WD=Q710197|lt=Baekje no Ōkimi clan|1=Kudara no Konikishi clan}}.
Mentions of "Shoban" appear in multiple entries in the ''{{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}},'' such as the 11th month of the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Seinei|en|Emperor Seinei|WD=Q357273|lt=Emperor Seinei|1=Emperor Seinei}}'s reign ({{ill|482|en|482|WD=Q32534|lt=482|1=482}}). During the [[Ritsuryō system]], administrative offices like the ''{{ill|Genbanryō|ja|玄蕃寮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Genbanryō|1=Q11572314|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11572314}}'' were established, and terms such as "Shoban," "Ban," and "Bankoku" were frequently used. After the unification of the Korean Peninsula by {{ill|Silla|en|Silla|WD=Q28456|lt=Silla|1=Silla}} in the latter half of the {{ill|7th century|en|7th century|WD=Q8089|lt=7th century|1=7th century}}, "Shoban" came to refer specifically to Silla in certain cases.
While the exact beginning of the usage of "Shoban" to denote immigrants is unclear, the annotation of the ''Nihon Shoki'' known as the "Kōnin Shiki" mentions the existence of a document titled ''Shoban Zatsuseiki'' ("Records of Various Foreign Names"). The earliest confirmed instance is in the ''{{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|Shinsen Shōjiroku|WD=Q1045362|lt=Shinsen Shōjiroku|1=Shinsen Shōjiroku}},'' compiled in {{ill|815|en|815|WD=Q29917|lt=815|1=815}} during the {{ill|Kōnin (era)|en|Kōnin (era)|WD=Q1373070|lt=Kōnin era|1=Kōnin (era)}}, where "Shoban" (not "Banbetsu") was used as a designation alongside ''{{ill|Kōbetsu|ja|皇別|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōbetsu|qq=}}'' and ''{{ill|Shinbetsu|ja|神別|1=Shinbetsu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbetsu|qq=}}.'' It was defined as referring to "clans of the Three Han."
In the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, under the [[kazoku]] system's {{ill|Clan system (Kazoku)|ja|宗族制 (華族)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clan system (Kazoku)|1=Q97312150|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q97312150}}, families of Shoban lineage were classified as "Gaibetsu" ("foreign origin").
